title: 17_top_products.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Label the Bars

Your chart of `top 5 products by units sold` came out with the right *shape* — five bars, descending heights — but the x-axis labels are `0, 1, 2, 3, 4`. The values are right; the labels are wrong. Your boss can't tell which bar is `mug` and which is `bag`.

The trap: `ax.bar(products, sales)` would label automatically, but you wrote `ax.bar(range(len(sales)), sales)` and forgot to tell the axis what to call those numeric positions. Use the right `bar()` arguments — or set the ticks afterwards.
initialCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

products = ["mug", "tee", "pen", "cap", "bag"]
sales    = [320, 280, 240, 200, 160]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.bar(range(len(sales)), sales, color="#E8B65A")
ax.set_title("top_products_q2.png")
ax.set_xlabel("product")
ax.set_ylabel("units sold")
plt.show()
traceback:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# x-axis labels are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — should be mug, tee, pen, cap, bag
correctOutput:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# bars labelled mug, tee, pen, cap, bag below the right heights

options.label: Pass the names to `bar()` directly: `ax.bar(products, sales, …)`
options.patchCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

products = ["mug", "tee", "pen", "cap", "bag"]
sales    = [320, 280, 240, 200, 160]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.bar(products, sales, color="#7CD992")
ax.set_title("top_products_q2.png")
ax.set_xlabel("product")
ax.set_ylabel("units sold")
plt.show()
options.resultLog:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# bars labelled mug, tee, pen, cap, bag — heights unchanged
options.rationale: When `bar()`'s first argument is a list of strings, matplotlib uses them as **categorical** x-positions: one bar per name, name printed below. No manual tick-setting needed. This is the cleanest fix and reads exactly like the code's intent.

options.label: Set the labels manually with `ax.set_xticklabels(products)`
options.patchCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

products = ["mug", "tee", "pen", "cap", "bag"]
sales    = [320, 280, 240, 200, 160]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.bar(range(len(sales)), sales, color="#E8B65A")
ax.set_xticklabels(products)
ax.set_title("top_products_q2.png")
ax.set_xlabel("product")
ax.set_ylabel("units sold")
plt.show()
options.resultLog:
UserWarning: FixedFormatter should only be used together with FixedLocator
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# labels render but matplotlib warns the ticks weren't fixed first
options.rationale: Works visually but raises the noisy `FixedFormatter should only be used together with FixedLocator` warning, because you set labels without setting the underlying tick positions. The correct two-line form is `ax.set_xticks(range(len(products))); ax.set_xticklabels(products)`. Letting `bar()` handle both is shorter and warning-free.

options.label: Rotate the existing numeric labels so they're easier to read
options.patchCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

products = ["mug", "tee", "pen", "cap", "bag"]
sales    = [320, 280, 240, 200, 160]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.bar(range(len(sales)), sales, color="#E8B65A")
ax.set_title("top_products_q2.png")
ax.set_xlabel("product")
ax.set_ylabel("units sold")
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=45)
plt.show()
options.resultLog:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# numbers 0..4 tilted 45° — still numbers, still useless
options.rationale: Rotates `0..4` to be more readable — but they're still `0..4`. The audience needs **what each bar is**, not a prettier presentation of placeholder numbers. The fix is to label them at all; pass the names to `bar()`.

hints: Look at `bar()`'s first argument. You wrote `range(len(sales))` — numeric positions. matplotlib will label whatever you hand it.
Pass `products` as the first argument: `ax.bar(products, sales, ...)`. matplotlib uses strings as categorical tick labels automatically.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`ax.bar(x, heights)` lays out one bar per `x` value. If `x` is numeric, the x-axis shows numbers; if `x` is a list of strings, matplotlib treats them as **categorical** and uses each string as the bar's tick label. The code passed `range(len(sales))`, which is `[0, 1, 2, 3, 4]` — so the axis honestly shows `0, 1, 2, 3, 4`. Nothing wrong with the heights; the labels just describe the positions you actually used.

### Why the fix is correct
Passing `products` as the first argument tells matplotlib "there are five categories named `mug`, `tee`, `pen`, `cap`, `bag`," and it draws one bar per category with the name underneath. No manual tick-setting, no warning, one line shorter. The general rule: **let `bar()` see the names you want to show.** Reach for manual `set_xticks` / `set_xticklabels` only when the positions and labels truly differ (e.g. logarithmic ticks with custom labels).
recruiterReview: Good — you saw that the bars were right and only the labels were wrong, then reached for the simplest correct fix (pass categories to `bar()`). The warning-emitting `set_xticklabels`-without-`set_xticks` mistake is one of matplotlib's most-Googled errors — knowing why it complains will save you when you do need manual tick control. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Labelling Bars

Getting the x-axis labels right is one of the smallest matplotlib skills that separates a notebook draft from something a stakeholder can read.

### The categorical shortcut
If the first argument to `bar()` is a list of strings, matplotlib treats them as categories and labels each bar with the corresponding string:
```python
ax.bar(["mug", "tee", "pen"], [320, 280, 240])
```
Three bars, labelled `mug`, `tee`, `pen`. No further work needed.

### The manual route — and why it's longer than it looks
If you pass numeric positions, the axis labels them with those numbers:
```python
ax.bar([0, 1, 2], [320, 280, 240])  # x-axis: 0, 1, 2
```
To override, you need to set **both** the tick positions and the labels:
```python
positions = [0, 1, 2]
labels    = ["mug", "tee", "pen"]
ax.bar(positions, [320, 280, 240])
ax.set_xticks(positions)        # fix the tick positions
ax.set_xticklabels(labels)      # then attach labels
```
Doing only `set_xticklabels` triggers matplotlib's `FixedFormatter should only be used together with FixedLocator` warning — it's reminding you that you set labels without committing to where the ticks live.

### When to use manual ticks
The categorical shortcut is right 95% of the time. Manual ticks are for:
- **Custom positions** — e.g. log-scale ticks at `[1, 10, 100]`.
- **Different label set than data** — e.g. plotting numeric `month_index` (`0..11`) and labelling with month abbreviations.
- **Long labels** that need rotation:
  ```python
  ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=45)
  ```

### Rotation, alignment, and overlap
When labels are long and overlap, three knobs help:
```python
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=45)
plt.setp(ax.get_xticklabels(), ha="right")  # right-align after rotation
fig.tight_layout()                          # let matplotlib expand for the labels
```
Without `tight_layout` rotated labels can be clipped at the bottom.

### Multiple bars per category (grouped bars)
When you have two series per category, you draw two `bar` calls with shifted x-positions:
```python
import numpy as np
products = ["mug", "tee", "pen"]
x = np.arange(len(products))
ax.bar(x - 0.2, q1, width=0.4, label="Q1")
ax.bar(x + 0.2, q2, width=0.4, label="Q2")
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(products)
ax.legend()
```
Numeric `x` is unavoidable here — but you set both ticks and labels.

### Common beginner mistakes
- `ax.bar(range(len(items)), values)` and being surprised by `0..N` labels.
- `set_xticklabels(...)` without `set_xticks(...)` and getting the formatter warning.
- Rotated labels falling off the figure — forgot `tight_layout`.

### You just learned…
Let `bar()` see the categories you want to display — pass them as the first argument. Manual tick control is for cases where positions and labels truly differ. When you do go manual, set both `set_xticks` and `set_xticklabels` together.

videos.title: Bar chart labels in matplotlib for beginners
videos.searchQuery: matplotlib bar chart x axis labels categorical tutorial

conceptCard:
This mission is about **how matplotlib decides x-axis labels**.

- Pass strings as the first arg to `bar()` → matplotlib labels each bar with the string.
- Pass numbers → matplotlib labels with those numbers.
- `set_xticklabels` alone warns; you need `set_xticks` too.

The code passes `range(len(sales))` so the bars are at `0..4` and the labels show `0..4`. Pass `products` instead.

lineNotes.noteMd: The category names — these are the labels we want on the x-axis.
The bar heights — these line up with `products` by position.
**The bug.** `range(len(sales))` is `[0, 1, 2, 3, 4]` — numeric positions. Replace with `products` so matplotlib uses the names.
`set_xlabel` is the **axis title** below the ticks — not the per-bar labels. Different thing.

takeaway: Pass categories (strings) to bar() — matplotlib labels each bar with the string for free.
